coredata.dc:title: Detection of tartrazine with fluorescence sensor from crayfish shell carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: He, Linjing
Du, Hongying
coredata.dc:description: As a typical water-soluble synthetic azo pigment, tartrazine is widely used in food products, and its abuse was harmful to our health. Hence, it is very important to establish a facile and economical method to detect the tartrazine content in food. In the current study, the crayfish shell carbon quantum dots (CS-CQDs) were prepared through a facile hydrothermal approach from crayfish shells. The CS-CQDs exhibited strong fluorescent emissions at 462 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The CS-CQDs were demonstrated to react with the synthetic pigments tartrazine selectively and effectively with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM in the linear range of 0–70 μM. The Stern-Volmer equation and thermodynamic analysis revealed that the fluorescence quenching was a spontaneous static quenching and mainly driven by van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. Furthermore, the CS-CQDs sensor was applied in energy beverage and durian candy samples with acceptable recovery of 92.7–104.5% and low RSD. Finally, we successfully designed a fluorescent test strip using CS-CQDs and filter paper. The current CS-CQDs had the potential ability to construct a simple paper strip for the detection of tartrazine in the future, which was useful for the food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent material
Pigment detection
Food
CS-CQDs
Fluorescent test strip

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity, repeatability and real food samples analysis
Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine, selectivity study and the corresponding mechanism
Thermodynamic analysis of the CS-CQDs binding with tartrazine
Repeatability and real food samples analysis and visual fluorescent test strip construction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CS-CQDs; (b) AFM image of CS-CQDs; (c) 3D AFM image of CS-CQDs; (d) particle size distribution diagram of CS-CQDs; (e) XRD patterns of CS-CQDs; (f) FT-IR spectrum of CS-CQDs; (g) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CS-CQDs; (h) XPS spectra of C1s; (i) XPS spectra of O1s; (j) XPS spectra of N1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of CS-CQDs (insert: photos of CS-CQDs under ultraviolet light of 365 nm and white light); (b) The fluorescence spectra of CS-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths; (c) Fluorescence decay curves of CS-CQDs; (d) Fluorescence decay curves of CS-CQDs+Tartrazine system.
(a) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs; (b) Effect of storage time on fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence intensities of CS-CQDs with different pigments; (b) Fluorescence quenching induced by different concentration of tartrazine; (c) Calibration curve of the fluorescence quenching versus tartrazine concentration of CS-CQDs; (d) The S-V plots for the quenching of CS-CQDs by tartrazine at various temperatures; (e) Calibration curve of the fluorescence quenching versus durian candy with spiked concentration of CS-CQDs; (f) Calibration curve of the fluorescence quenching versus energy beverage with spiked concentration of CS-CQDs; (g) Photos of CS-CQDs test strip with different concentrations of tartrazine under ultraviolet light of 365 nm.
Comparison of the proposed method with reported methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Data with different superscript letters in the same column are significantly different (P < 0.05)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test of the real samples.

head.ce:title: Detection of tartrazine with fluorescence sensor from crayfish shell carbon quantum dots

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing − original draft
Supervision, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing − review & editing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology, Huazhong Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a typical water-soluble synthetic azo pigment, tartrazine is widely used in food products, and its abuse was harmful to our health. Hence, it is very important to establish a facile and economical method to detect the tartrazine content in food. In the current study, the crayfish shell carbon quantum dots (CS-CQDs) were prepared through a facile hydrothermal approach from crayfish shells. The CS-CQDs exhibited strong fluorescent emissions at 462 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The CS-CQDs were demonstrated to react with the synthetic pigments tartrazine selectively and effectively with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM in the linear range of 0–70 μM. The Stern-Volmer equation and thermodynamic analysis revealed that the fluorescence quenching was a spontaneous static quenching and mainly driven by van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. Furthermore, the CS-CQDs sensor was applied in energy beverage and durian candy samples with acceptable recovery of 92.7–104.5% and low RSD. Finally, we successfully designed a fluorescent test strip using CS-CQDs and filter paper. The current CS-CQDs had the potential ability to construct a simple paper strip for the detection of tartrazine in the future, which was useful for the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, dyes and pigments pollution has caused some serious ecological issues and aroused wide concern (Yousefi et al., 2015; Yousefi et al., 2021b). Tartrazine, a typical water-soluble synthetic azo pigment, is widely used in beverages, candies, ice cream sauces and other food industry products (Xu et al., 2015). Furthermore, it is also used in medications, personal care and cosmetics field to blend the color of products (Wang and Zhao, 2015). In general, azo pigments are typically cleared by kidneys and liver in human body (Lei et al., 2013). However, some studies have reported that the excessive intake of tartrazine may cause adverse health effects, such as diarrhea, migraine, carcinogenicity. Furthermore, it also damages the function of kidney and liver (Huang et al., 2021; Sakthivel et al., 2018). Thus, many scholars pay attention to the detection and degradation of dyes (Mahdi et al., 2022; Yousefi et al., 2021a; Yousefi et al., 2021c). The detection of pigments is of great importance for the bio-safety and future industrial processing. Hence, it is very important to establish a simple and economical method to detect the tartrazine content in foods.

Currently, many analytical methods have been established for the detection of tartrazine, such as high performance liquid chromatography (Alves et al., 2008), electrochemical analysis (State et al., 2022), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Jang et al., 2021), FT-IR spectrometry (Amsaraj and Mutturi, 2021), image (RGB) analysis(de Carvalho Oliveira et al., 2022) and fluorometry (Chatzimitakos et al., 2017). However, most of them suffer from some disadvantages in routine detection, such as complicated pretreatment, sophisticated equipments and long detection period (Nebot et al., 2013; Zoughi et al., 2021). Among these methods, the fluorescence method was convenient for detection of actual samples due to its advantages of easy sample preparation, convenient operation and shorter detection period. Even compared with rapid methods such as FT-IR and image methods, fluorescence method has less data processing and better limit of detection (Amsaraj and Mutturi, 2021; Vidal et al., 2018). Among the fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as new fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which are widely used in sensing, bioimaging, catalytic and drug delivery due to good water solubility, stable photostability, low toxicity and great biocapacity (Chellasamy et al., 2022).

Moreover, CQDs have attracted particular attention as fluorescence sensors in the food safety field, including the detection of metal ions, drug residue, food additives, food pathogens, nutrients and other related researches (Zhang et al., 2022). Boobalan et al. synthesized the CQDs from mushroom to detect Pb+ with the LOD of 58.63 μM in the linear range of 10–200 μM (Boobalan et al., 2020); Yang et al. prepared CQDs from xylan, which could identify tetracycline with a detection limit of 6.49 nM, suggesting a potential methods for the sensing of tetracycline in biomedical field (Yang et al., 2018); Su et al. designed carbon dots from dried lemon peel for detecting carmine in soft drinks samples, and the LOD was 0.16 mg/L and the recoveries of soft drinks samples were 97.1–103.2% (Su et al., 2018); Wang et al. synthesized carbon dots using papaya powder as a precursor for the detection of E. coli, and the LOD was 9.5 × 104 CFU/mL in the range of 105-108 CFU/mL (Wang et al., 2016).

Crayfish is a very popular food sources in China. In 2021, the total production of crayfish breeding in China has reached 2.6336 million tons, ranking the sixth in freshwater breeding varieties in China (information from China crayfish industry development report 2022). In the traditional processing process, about 80% of the crayfish shells have been discarded as waste (Peng et al., 2016). Crayfish shell contain three basic compounds, chitin (20%−30%), protein (20%−30%) and calcium carbonate (30%−40%), which made it a good precursor of CQDs (Chen et al., 2020).

Thus, we demonstrated the utilization of crayfish shells in hydrothermal synthetic procedures to prepare crayfish shell carbon quantum dots (CS-CQDs) without any chemicals in the current study. The CS-CODs were further used to the detection of tartrazine in food products and developed a visual test strip, which could be useful for the future rapid detection.

The crayfish (bred in captivity) was purchased from the aquatic product market of Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, China, and the detected energy beverage and durian candy were purchased from the local supermarket in Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, China. Drugs of tartrazine, sunset yellow, carmine, amaranth, bright blue and indigo were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China), and all chemicals were analytical reagent grade. The qualitative filter paper without fluorescence purchased from ADVANTEC (Japan). All water in the experiment is distilled water.

The HRTEM (High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope) images were taken from the Transmission Electron Microscope (JEM-2100 F, JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired using a Dimension Icon AFM (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was obtained using the Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) identified the functionalities of CS-CQDs using Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS 50, Thermo Scientific, USA). The chemical bond and composition were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments, which were carried out using Kα XPS Analysis Instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA). UV–visible absorption spectrum was conducted with the UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV2600A, Unico, China). Fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of fluorescence experiments were performed using a FL1000 steady state and lifetime fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The CS-CQDs were readily synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment. The crayfish shells (∼1 g, including crayfish pincers, dried and grinded after cleaning) were taken and dispersed in 25 mL distilled water. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept in air oven for 5.5 h at 180 ºC. After the air cooling, the brownish solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and filtered by a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove the sediments. Then, the filtrate was dialyzed through 500 D dialysis membranes for 24 h to further remove the impurities. Finally, the CS-CQDs solid powder were obtained by freeze-drying for 24 h, and the CS-CQDs powder was further dissolved into the distilled water to obtain the desired concentration during the detection procedure.

The stability of CS-CQDs under different storage periods was examined, and the pH dependence fluorescence of CS-CQDs was analyzed in the Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer which was prepared according to a previous preparation method (Ding et al., 2021). The B-R buffer consists of H3BO3 (0.0443 M), CH3COOH (0.0413 M) and H3PO4 (0.0398 M). The pH value of the solution was adjusted with NaOH (1 M) and the fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs under different pH values between 3 and 9 was recorded. Besides, the CS-CQDs were stored at 4 ºC for 10 days, and the fluorescence intensity were recorded every two days to observe the fluorescence changes with different storage periods.

For the fluorescent analysis, the original CS-CQDs solution was diluted and used, the final concentration is ∼2 mg/mL. Then, the detected CS-CQDs solution (1.5 mL) and an equal volume of tartrazine solution with different concentrations (0–100 μM) were mixed together in a cuvette. After 5 min reaction, the fluorescent spectra were collected at 380 nm excitation wavelength.

Moreover, to investigate the binding mechanism of the interaction between CS-CQDs and tartrazine, the quenching fluorescent spectra were recorded at 298 K, 308 K and 318 K under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The concentration of CS-CQDs is the same as before, and the tartrazine concentrations were ranged from 0–100 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, repeatability and real food samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In the current study, there were six synthetic pigments involved for the selective study, such as tartrazine, sunset yellow, carmine, amaranth, bright blue and indigo. The detected CS-CQDs solution (∼2 mg/mL, 1.5 mL,) was mixed with different kinds of pigment solution (50 μM, 1.5 mL). After 5 min incubation, the fluorescent spectra were recorded under 380 nm excitation wavelength. The intra-day and inter-day RSD were obtained by successive measurements (n = 5) of tartrazine at different concentrations (5, 10, 20 μM) to evaluate the repeatability of the CS-CQDs fluorescent methods. Besides, two food samples (energy beverage and durian candy, tartrazine is the only pigment in their compositions) were utilized for the real sample detection. The energy beverage was purchased from the local supermarket, and no extra processing was performed after opening the bottle; and the durian candy was also purchased from the local supermarket, and 10 g durian candy was dissolved into 100 mL distilled water at 70 ºC. The resulting solution was filtered by a 0.22 µm syringe filter for future detection. After 5 min reaction time of the food sample solution and CS-CQDs, the fluorescent spectra were measured to evaluate the content of tartrazine in these samples, and the detected results were compared with the results from the Chinese national standard method and linear regression results. Besides, different tartrazine solution (4 and 8 μM) was prepared and added to the sample solution to obtained the spiked recoveries.

Typically, the filter paper (qualitative filter paper without fluorescence) was sliced into small square strips (∼1 ×1 cm2) and dipped into the CS-CQD solution (4 mg/mL, prepared from CS-CQDs powder and distilled water) at room temperature. Specifically, five pieces of filter paper were immersed in 50 mL of CS-CQDs aqueous solution. After 5 min, the stripes were pulled out and naturally dried at room temperature. Then, different concentrations of tartrazine solution (0–100 μM, 20 μL) were prepared and dropped on the strip with a pipette. After 5 min’ reaction, the fluorescence of the test strips was observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light in dark environment.

The figures were drawn using Origin 2022 (OriginLab, USA); and the peak-differentiation and imitating of XPS data were carried out using Thermo Avantage v5.9922 (Thermo Scientific, USA). The statistical analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel (Microsoft, USA) and SPSS 22.0 (IBM, USA) and the significant differences between the means (P＜0.05) were determined by one-way ANOVA test. For all the studies, the values were presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

The morphology and size of the CS-CQDs were investigated with HRTEM and AFM. The HRTEM images indicated that the synthesized CS-CQDs were nearly spherical (
Fig. 1a). The inserted figure in Fig. 1a showed that the lattice spacing was around 0.12 nm, which reveals an amorphous carbon phase of the CS-CQDs. The particle sizes of CS-CQDs were acquired and illustrated with distribution diagram, and it was found that the CS-CQDs have an average particle size of 2.85 nm (Fig. 1d). Similarly, the AFM images further demonstrated that the CS-CQDs were well dispersed and quasi-spherical (Fig. 1b-c). The XRD pattern of the CS-CQDs (Fig. 1e) showed a broad peak appears at 2θ = 22.1 °, which also corresponds to the amorphous carbon phase. The results also supported the lattice spacing in TEM. Liang et al. synthesized CQDs from hemicellulose (by-product of plant refinery) via a one-step hydrothermal method, and their CQDs also illustrated small particle size and amorphous carbon phase (Liang et al., 2014).

The FT-IR spectrum for CS-CQDs was shown in Fig. 1f and demonstrated the surface functional groups in CS-CQDs. The peaks at 3391 cm−1 and 2959 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H and C-H. They were hydrophilic and made the synthesized CS-CQDs with high hydrophilicity and strong solubility in water. The water solubility of most CQDs was also due to the hydrophilic groups in different studies (Li et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022). The strong peak at 1657 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO or CN. The peak at 1404 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C-N and C-O-C, respectively. The FT-IR results showed that the CS-CQDs contains N functional groups, which indicated the nitrogen from crayfish shell had self-doped on CQDs.

Moreover, the XPS was used to confirm and analyze the chemical compositions of CS-CQDs. The XPS spectra (Fig. 1g) showed three obvious peaks at 531.21, 399.75 and 284.81 eV, which were attributed to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively. The inserted figure demonstrated the fractions of these three elements, C1s: N1s: O1s = 65.8%: 15.5%: 18.7%. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) was deconvoluted and fitted into four peaks at 284.8, 285.7, 286.3 and 288.1 eV which represented four carbon bonds: CC/C-C, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively. These indicated the abundant N and O functionalities on the CS-CQDs framework. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 1i) comprised skeletal C-OH (531.3 eV) and C-O-C/O-H (532.3 eV). The deconvoluted N1s spectrum (Fig. 1j) contained three peaks at 398.5, 399.9 and 401.6 eV, which were attributed to C-N-C, C-N, and N-H, respectively. Thus, the XPS and FT-IR analysis exhibited abundant functional groups in CS-CQDs, such as -OH, -NH on CS-CQDs, which facilitated the binding between CQDs and other materials.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were used to characterize the optical properties of CS-CQDs. In the UV-Vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. 2a), the peaks centered at 265 and 335 nm could be attributed to the π→π * transition of the double bonds and the n→π * transition of double bonds of CO, CN, et al. (Zhang et al., 2019). Besides, the max emission peak of CS-CQDs appeared at 462 nm under the 380 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 2a), and the inserted figure illustrated that the color of CS-CQDs aqueous solution appeared yellow in sunlight but emitted bright blue under ultraviolet light of 365 nm. The fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths were illustrated in Fig. 2b. It could be observed that the emission peaks showed red shifted at excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 450 nm. The excitation dependent features could be attributed to the different particle sizes and considerable surface defects caused by oxygen and nitrogen functional groups (Raja et al., 2021). Fig. 2c demonstrated the fluorescence decay and triexponential fitting curves of CS-CQDs. According to the tribble exponential simulation, the average fluorescence lifetime of CS-CQDs was calculated to be 6.75 ns. Furthermore, the quantum yield (QY) of CS-CQDs was 9.33%, which was higher than a number of biomass-based CQDs. For example, Chen et al. reported CQDs synthesized with waste tea and the relevant QY was 7.1% (Chen et al., 2019), and Zhang et al. prepared fish scale carbon dots through hydrothermal reaction with 6.9% quantum yield (Zhang et al., 2019).

The stability of CS-CQDs was measured under different pH environments and storage times before being used as a fluorescence probe in practical detection. The CS-CQDs aqueous solution was stored at 4 ºC for a 10-day period and the fluorescence intensity was measured every two days. It appeared that the fluorescence changes very slightly during the whole storage period ( Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3b, there was no obvious changes for the fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs in the different pH environments from 3 to 9, which indicated that the CS-CQDs were basically stable in different acidity solutions. The stability of storage and pH suggested that the CS-CQDs could be suitable for sensing and application in various environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of tartrazine, selectivity study and the corresponding mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity and performance of CS-CQDs in detecting tartrazine, different pigments aqueous solution (tartrazine, sunset yellow, carmine, amaranth, bright blue and indigo, 50 μM) was utilized for the selectivity study. As shown in Fig. 4a, tartrazine had the strongest fluorescence response among these six pigments. Besides, the tartrazine with increasing concentration (0–100 μM) caused strong fluorescent intensities quenching of CS-CQDs continuously, which was shown in Fig. 4b. The relationship between various concentrations of tartrazine and the varies fluorescent intensities was calculated and illustrated in Fig. 4c, and the linear equation is Y = 0.0249 X + 0.9374 with the R2 = 0.996. The linear plot explained that there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching and tartrazine concentration (0 μM to 70 μM). The LOD was calculated using the following equation: LOD = 3σ ⁄ m, where σ (0.00384) is the relative standard deviation and m (0.0249) is assigned to the slope of the linear calibration curve. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.46 μM. Due to the limit of tartrazine required by Chinese national standard (GB 2760–2014) in the beverages is 0.1 g/kg (about 187 μM) and the limit in dairy, ice cream and jelly is 0.05 g/kg (about 93.5 μM), which means that CS-CQDs fluorescence sensor in the current study could be used for the tartrazine content detection in food samples.

A comparison of those different reported methods has shown in Table 1. Although the LOD of our method is not the lowest in the table, CS-CQDs gives a wider linear range compared to fluorescence methods presented in the Table 1. As for other methods with special equipment and sophisticated operations, our LOD method has shown remarkable advantages.

In order to investigate the binding mechanism of the interaction between CS-CQDs and tartrazine, the fluorescent spectrum was analyzed. The fluorescence quenching types were usually classified as static quenching and dynamic quenching by their dependence on whether a steady-state complex was formed (Xu et al., 2022). It could be distinguished by the quenching constant at different temperatures. For the static quenching, the quenching constant decreases with increasing temperature, but the opposite effect was observed for the dynamic quenching. The fluorescence quenching of the CS-CQDs by tartrazine could be described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1) (Lakowicz and Weber, 1973):
(1)
F
0
/
F
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
Q
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
K
q
=
K
SV
/
τ
0
Where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CS-CQDs in the absence and presence of the tartrazine, respectively. K
q designates the quenching rate constant and [Q] denotes the concentration of tartrazine. τ
0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs. τ
0 of the CS-CQDs is 6.754 ns. Besides, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

The Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 4d) at various temperatures (298, 308 and 318 K) demonstrated a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and increasing tartrazine concentration. As it could be seen from Table 2, the calculated K
q of CS-CQDs at three different temperatures were 3.95 ± 0.031 × 1012 (298 K), 3.85 ± 0.029 × 1012 (308 K) and 3.58 ± 0.021 × 1012 (318 K) L/mol/s, respectively. All the K
q values were higher than the maximum scattering collision quenching constant (2.0 ×1010 L/mol/s) (Wang et al., 2021). Furthermore, K
SV value was gradually decreased with temperature (298–318 K), indicating that the fluorescence quenching was arisen from the CS-CQDs-tartrazine system and the process was belonging to the static quenching. The fluorescence decay results confirmed this conclusion again. Upon incubation with tartrazine, the fluorescence decay curve of CS-CQDs and tartrazine was almost coincides with the curve of CS-CQDs. Since there was no obvious change in fluorescence lifetime (6.75 ns and 6.70 ns, Fig. 2c-d) observed with adding tartrazine, it confirmed that tartrazine had no influence on the relaxation time of the CS-CQDs electrons of excitation state, and the fluorescence quenching was static quenching mechanism. These results were consist with the phenomenon in a former study of the blue fluorescence sulfur quantum dots designed by Peng et al. (Peng et al., 2022). The fluorescence lifetimes of the CQDs for their study were 9.52 ns and 9.31 ns in the absence and presence of tartrazine, thus denying that the fluorescence quenching was a dynamic quenching. All the above phenomena indicated that the fluorescence of CS-CQDs was statically quenched by adding tartrazine. Therefore, CS-CQDs interacted with the quencher tartrazine to form a non-fluorescent ground state complex. The complex of CS-CQDs and tartrazine could absorb light and return to the ground state without emitting photons, which resulted a decrease in CS-CQDs fluorescence (Zu et al., 2017).

For the binding interaction of CS-CQDs with tartrazine, the binding constants (K
a ) and binding sites (n) were determined by the double logarithmic Eq. (2).
(2)
lg
F
0
/
F
− 1
= lg
K
a
+ n lg
Q
Where K
a is designated as binding constant, n denotes the number of binding sites.

The calculated results of the binding constant (K
a ) and binding site (n) were listed in Table 2. K
a values decreased with the increasing temperature, which indicated that the stability of the CS-CQDs-Tartrazine complex decreased. Even so, the K
a values at three temperatures were in the order of 104 and 105 M−1. It could be suggested there existed a strong binding force in the interaction between CS-CQDs and tartrazine (Shen et al., 2015). The n values at the temperature ranges were close to 1, which indicated that CS-CQDs had a single binding site combined with tartrazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic analysis of the CS-CQDs binding with tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para:
Analysis of the thermodynamic parameters could help determining the binding force of the CS-CQDs-tartrazine system. Typically, there were four types of interaction binding force between ligands and molecules, including hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic bonding and electrostatic as well as van der Waals forces (Fathi et al., 2019). For the hydrophobic interaction, the thermodynamic parameters of the reaction were ΔH > 0, ΔS > 0. Both ΔH and ΔS were negative indicating there were hydrogen bonds and a van der Waals in the system. Besides, ΔH < 0, ΔS > 0 indicated the electrostatic force (Yu et al., 2022). The thermodynamic parameters could be evaluated using the Van't Hoff Eqs. (3) and (4).
(3)
lg
K
a
= − (
∆ H
/
R ∙ T )
+
∆ S
/
R
(4)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S
Where R denotes gas constant (8.314 J/mol/K). T is absolute temperature, ΔH is enthalpy change. ΔG is the change in free energy, ΔS is designated as the change entropy.

All the thermodynamic parameters for the binding of CS-CQDs and tartrazine were collected in Table 2. From Table 2, the negative values of ΔH (−34.6 kJ/mol) and ΔS (−15.8 J/mol/K) indicated that CS-CQDs might bind to tartrazine by means of van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bond. This might be attributed that CS-CQDs were rich in -OH, -COOH and N-H groups, which could be formed into the hydrogen bond with nitrogen and oxygen atoms in tartrazine. These were useful for the interaction between CS-CQDs and tartrazine and the mixture was quickly formed a complex by the hydrogen bonding when they are close. Similar conclusion was also obtained in the interaction between tartrazine and Apo-transferrin in a former study (Jafari-Arvari et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability and real food samples analysis and visual fluorescent test strip construction
ce:section.ce:para: The intra-day and inter-day repeatability of the CS-CQDs fluorescent methods was assessed by successive detection (n = 5) of tartrazine at different concentrations (5, 10, 20 μM). The CS-CQDs fluorescent methods exhibited good repeatability with good RSD values of 1.97% (5 μM), 2.71% (10 μM), 1.61% (20 μM) in intra-day and 1.12% (5 μM), 2.99% (10 μM), 2.38% (20 μM) in inter-day. Then, in order to evaluate the detection capability of tartrazine content of the current CS-CQDs in the real food samples, durian candy and energy beverage were adopted for further analysis. The real samples were spiked with tartrazine solutions with different concentrations (0, 4 and 8 μM). As can be seen in Table 3, the recovery at different concentrations were ranged from 92.7% to 105%, and the RSDs were kept between 1.51% and 4.62%, which was an adopted level compared to the other similar studies. Zoughi et al. prepared CDs based citric acid to detect tartrazine in Saffron tea with the recovery of 97.4%− 104.1% (Zoughi et al., 2021). Xu et al. synthesized CDs from aloe to detect tartrazine in honey, candy and steamed buns, and the recovery range extended from 88.6%− 103.4%, and the RSDs were ranged from 0.2%− 3.9%. Yang et al. designed N, Cl-Fluorescence CDs for the detection of tartrazine in beverage samples, and the recoveries were ranged from 99.7% to 100.5% and RSDs were ranged from 1.95% and 3.75%. Moreover, the results of CS-CQDs fluorescence methods were basically consistent with the results of Chinese national standard method GB 4481.1–2010 and linear regression results. As shown in Fig. 4e-f, the linear regression analysis showed the tartrazine concentration of original durian candy and beverage (0 μM spiked concentration) is 8.05 and 2.27, respectively. These results are close to results from the national standard method. These results verified the potential and reliability of CS-CQDs fluorescence probe in the determination of tartrazine in real food samples. However, the specificity of the current CODs should be the limitations during the application, as the CS-CODs is not selective to tartrazine. However, it was applicable for the detection of tartrazine in the real samples with the only pigment to avoid the interference problems. Thus, the specificity of the future CODs should be solved in our future study.

Furthermore, the liquid CS-CQDs fluorescence sensor was not convenient during the practical detection. Therefore, the visual fluorescent test strip was constructed by CS-CQDs and filter paper in the current study. As shown in Fig. 4g, the fluorescence of the strip was dimmed, and the color of the strip changed to slightly yellow from blue with the increasing concentrations of tartrazine. The general visual detection of tartrazine was realized by the color change of the test strips under ultraviolet light of 365 nm. Thus, the current CS-CQDs had the potential ability to construct a simple paper strip for the detection of tartrazine in the future, which was useful for the food industry.

In summary, the current work demonstrated a simple, economical and environment-friendly hydrothermal method to synthesis CS-CQDs fluorescence sensor from crayfish shell to detect tartrazine. The morphology, chemical compositions and optical properties of CS-CQDs were thoroughly investigated for further application, and the CS-CQDs exhibited good performance in the detection of tartrazine with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM in the linear range of 0–70 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescent spectra and thermodynamic analysis revealed the fluorescence quenching of CS-CQDs and tartrazine was a spontaneous static quenching which mainly driven by van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds. The developed fluorescence probe was used to detect the tartrazine concentrations in candy and beverage samples with acceptable recovery of 92.7–104.5% and low RSD (1.51% and 4.62%), which confirmed the potential of practical application. Finally, to overcome the limitations of aqueous sensing systems, a novel visual fluorescent test strip was successfully constructed. Thus, the current CS-CODs had the potential ability to construct a simple paper strip for the detection of tartrazine in the future, which was useful for the food industry.

Linjing He: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing − original draft. Hongying Du: Supervision, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Key R & D Program of China (No. 2019YFC1606003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31772047).
